problem_summary: 2014 MCM Problem C ("Who are the 20%?") asks teams to use network-science / measurement techniques to identify the most influential ~20% of nodes in research networks. The paper works the official Erdos1 co-author dataset (511 authors) for Requirements 1-2 (rank most-influential and best-connected researchers, characterize the network), a citation network of foundational network-science papers for Requirement 3 (rank papers by influence), a Hollywood co-star network for Requirement 4 (extend the method to a different domain), and a discussion of scale-free dynamics plus an influence-boosting strategy for Requirement 5. The team built two reusable models — a Relation Distance Model (SNA centralities + TOPSIS-style distance) and an Authority-Popularity Evaluation Model (modified PageRank + citations/year) — and applied them across all four networks.
models_used: Relation Distance Model: a TOPSIS-style ranking that builds a 3-D measure vector per node from normalized degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality, defines an ideal 'most-influential' vector (1,1,0), and ranks nodes by Euclidean distance D_C(x) to that ideal (smaller = more influential). Used for Req 1 on the 511-node Erdos1 co-author network; top node = HARARY, FRANK.

Eigenvector centrality (computed in UCINET) as a second, independent ranking for Req 2 that weights a node by both its degree and the importance of its neighbors; top node = ALON, NOGA M. Used as cross-validation against the Relation Distance ranking.

Three SNA centrality measures as building blocks: degree centrality, betweenness centrality (shortest-path counts), and closeness centrality, with all-pairs shortest paths computed via the Floyd(-Warshall) algorithm.

Authority-Popularity Evaluation Model: a 2-axis influence scheme combining a 'depth' axis (Authority Index from a Modified PageRank) and a 'width' axis (Popularity Index = citations per year), plotted on an Authority-Popularity diagram split into four quadrants to pick the globally most influential node. Req 3 winner = paper No.14 (Watts & Strogatz, 'Collective dynamics of small-world networks').

Modified PageRank Algorithm (Req 3): standard PageRank with damping q=0.85 but (a) initialized with non-uniform priors = normalized journal impact factors instead of uniform 1/N, and (b) handling of the 3 dangling nodes (out-degree 0) in the 24-paper citation network; iterated to convergence threshold max|PR(k)-PR(k-1)| < 0.0001 in MATLAB.

Weighted/Improved PageRank (Req 4): PageRank variant where edge weights (IMDb ratings of co-starred movies) determine how a node distributes its rank to neighbors (proportional split rather than even split); applied to a 15-node directed Hollywood co-star network. Winner = Angelina Jolie.

Power-law / scale-free network characterization: fit the Erdos1 degree distribution to a power law P(K>k) ~ k^-1.6 using the Clauset (2007) maximum-likelihood + goodness-of-fit method, plus clustering coefficient (0.343) and average path length (3.825); interpreted via the Barabasi-Albert growth + preferential-attachment model.
Sensitivity analysis: swept the PageRank damping coefficient q from 0.3 to 1.0 and measured ranking 'similarity' to the q=0.85 baseline to demonstrate robustness.
key_techniques: Reduced subjective multi-index ranking to a single scalar via a TOPSIS-style Euclidean distance to an explicit ideal vector (1,1,0), with each centrality min-max normalized to [0,1] first so the three axes are comparable.

Used TWO independent ranking methods (Relation Distance vs Eigenvector centrality) and explicitly compared the top-15 lists as internal cross-validation, then diagnosed the disagreement (HARARY ranks #1 by distance but lower by eigenvector because his 44 co-authors are mostly low-influence) to expose a real model weakness.

Separated 'influence' into two orthogonal dimensions — depth (authority via PageRank) and width (reach via citations/year) — instead of collapsing to one number, then used a 2-D quadrant plot to read off the dominant node.
Injected domain priors into PageRank initialization: journal impact factor as the per-paper prior weight rather than the default uniform prior, grounding the algorithm in real bibliometric signal.

Adapted the same core algorithm to three structurally different networks: weight-on-nodes (citation network) vs weight-on-edges (co-star network), and undirected (co-author) vs directed (citation/co-star) — showing one toolkit transfers across Requirements 1-4.

Explicitly handled PageRank failure modes — dangling nodes (out-degree 0) and the 'rich-get-too-rich' collapse — and justified the damping coefficient q both mathematically and via a domain story (some movies don't cast the biggest star).

Validated the network model class with a rigorous statistical fit: Clauset et al. MLE power-law fitting (not naive log-log regression) to justify the scale-free claim, backed by clustering coefficient and average path length.

Tied Requirement 5 back to the problem's own framing (the 80-20 / Pareto principle and the Matthew effect) and turned analysis into an actionable strategy: find shortest paths through the approachable 80% (low closeness to the target) to eventually co-author with the key 20% node.

Clear, reproducible data-preprocessing pipeline (Excel countif to extract 511 Erdos1 authors and strip Erdos2, then MATLAB to build the 511x511 adjacency matrix), with explicit ID-numbering convention.
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins on four fronts. (1) Coverage with coherence: it answers all five requirements but does so with just two reusable models applied consistently, so the paper reads as one unified method rather than five disconnected mini-solutions. (2) Methodological credibility: it doesn't just invoke centrality — it borrows the TOPSIS ideal-point idea to fuse indices, uses the rigorous Clauset MLE power-law test (a genuinely sophisticated choice for 2014 undergraduates) to back the scale-free claim, and correctly identifies and fixes PageRank's dangling-node problem. (3) Intellectual honesty: it runs two independent rankings, notices they disagree, and uses the disagreement to surface a true limitation (distance model is over-sensitive to raw degree) — judges reward teams that interrogate their own results. (4) Generalization and insight: the same machinery extends cleanly from mathematicians to papers to movie stars, and Requirement 5 connects the math back to the problem's headline metaphor (80-20, Matthew effect) with a concrete, original 'shortcut to boost influence' strategy. A clean preprocessing pipeline, a damping-coefficient sensitivity sweep, and a candid strengths/weaknesses section round out the rigor judges look for."
section_highlights: "The summary sheet is well-engineered: it names both models up front and maps each one to specific requirements, so a judge knows the plan in 30 seconds. The strongest structural move is the requirement-driven organization (Section 4 = Req 1-2, Section 5 = Req 3-4, Section 6 = Req 5) with each model introduced once and then reused, which keeps the narrative tight. Section 4.3 (Validation of the Model) is a standout: rather than burying the degree-sensitivity flaw, it dedicates a subsection to the HARARY case to explain WHY the two rankings differ. The Authority-Popularity quadrant diagram (Figure 6/9) is an effective visual that lets the reader literally see the most-influential node in the top-right corner. The iteration flowchart (Figure 5) and the edge-weight redistribution diagram (Figure 8) make the PageRank variants concrete. Assumptions are stated explicitly and each is justified, the symbol table is complete, and the sensitivity analysis (Figure 10) plus an explicit Strengths/Weaknesses subsection signal mature self-assessment."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The Relation Distance Model is over-sensitive to raw degree (the team admits this): a high-degree node like HARARY ranks #1 even though most of his co-authors are low-influence, so the 'distance-to-ideal' ranking can be inflated by quantity over quality of connections.

The ideal vector (1,1,0) and the unweighted Euclidean distance implicitly give the three centralities equal weight with no justification or weight-sensitivity test — the choice of equal weighting is arbitrary.

Tiny, hand-curated samples for Requirements 3-4: only 24 papers (16 given + 8 self-found) and 15 hand-picked movie stars. The team even concedes the model needs large data to be accurate ('if the data of a network is limited, the error is large'), so the citation and co-star rankings rest on thin, selection-biased samples.

Ground-truth proxies are weak and somewhat ad hoc: popularity measured by raw Google search-result counts (name-collision prone, e.g. common names) and movie quality by IMDb rating; citations/year mixes data of differing recency. No external validation that the produced rankings match expert consensus beyond the internal two-method comparison.
Assumption 1 (all co-authorships have equal strength, 0/1) discards collaboration intensity (number of joint papers), which the richer Erdos data could have supported.

Power-law exponent 1.6 is stated but the goodness-of-fit p-value from the Clauset test is not reported, so the scale-free claim is asserted more than quantified; the degree-distribution figure also looks coarse.

The OCR'd figures/tables in this markdown are partly garbled (network-graph value tables, the citation-network node list), so some numeric details are unreliable as transcribed — a reminder that the substantive contribution lives in the models, not the auto-extracted tables.
reusable_patterns: TOPSIS-style ranking template: normalize several competing indices to [0,1], define an explicit ideal point, and rank by Euclidean distance to it — a clean, defensible way to fuse multi-criteria scores into one ranking for ANY MCM problem with multiple quality metrics.

Two-independent-rankings-then-reconcile pattern: compute the answer two different ways, compare top-k lists, and turn any disagreement into a diagnosed limitation. This single move simultaneously validates results and demonstrates intellectual honesty to judges.

Depth-vs-width decomposition: split a fuzzy concept like 'influence' into two orthogonal measurable axes and use a 2-D quadrant plot to identify the dominant element — transferable to importance/risk/priority ranking problems.

Domain-prior PageRank: replace PageRank's uniform initialization with a meaningful prior (impact factor, rating, population, GDP) so the algorithm is anchored in real signal; and switch between node-weighted and edge-weighted PageRank depending on where the data lives.

Algorithm-failure-mode checklist: explicitly identify and patch dangling nodes / sinks / the rich-get-richer collapse, and justify the damping/regularization parameter both mathematically and with a domain story.

Mandatory robustness sweep: vary the one tunable parameter (here q) across its range and report a similarity-to-baseline curve to claim stability — a low-effort, high-credibility section to include in any iterative model.
Use the rigorous statistical test, not the naive one: when claiming a distribution (power-law / scale-free), use Clauset-style MLE + goodness-of-fit rather than eyeballing a log-log plot.

Generalize across domains to prove robustness: deliberately re-run the same model on a deliberately different network (math co-authors -> citations -> movie stars) to demonstrate the method is a general tool, which strongly impresses judges on interdisciplinary (ICM-style) problems.

Tie the conclusion back to the problem's own framing: connect your math to the headline concept the prompt used (here Pareto 80-20 and the Matthew effect) and deliver an actionable strategy, not just a ranking.
Document a reproducible preprocessing pipeline with an explicit ID convention so results are traceable from raw data to final table.
